event: During the African Humid Period the Sahara is a green savanna, and early populations leave rock art and settlements in what is now Mauritania.
Neolithic herding communities flourish around lakes and grasslands, leaving pottery, tools, and cattle imagery before the Sahara begins drying out.
Progressive desertification of the Sahara pushes populations southward and toward oases, reshaping settlement across the region.
Berber (Amazigh) peoples gradually migrate into and across the western Sahara, becoming dominant among the region's nomadic populations.
Trans-Saharan exchange networks develop as the domesticated camel begins to transform desert travel and trade.
The Ghana Empire (Wagadou) emerges to the south, controlling gold-and-salt trade routes that pass through southern Mauritania.
Arab-Muslim expansion across North Africa begins introducing Islam toward the western Sahara via Berber intermediaries.
Aoudaghost, a key southern Saharan trading town in present-day Mauritania, becomes a major caravan hub linked to the Ghana Empire.
The Almoravid movement is founded among the Sanhaja Berbers of the western Sahara, launching a militant Islamic reform drive.
Almoravid forces seize Aoudaghost, expanding their control over the trans-Saharan trade routes.
Almoravids reportedly capture the Ghana Empire's capital, accelerating the spread of Islam through the region.
Chinguetti and Ouadane rise as Saharan trading and religious centers, becoming renowned hubs of Islamic learning and caravan commerce.
The Almoravid dynasty collapses under pressure from the Almohads, ending its empire spanning the Maghreb and Iberia.
The Mali Empire rises to dominate the southern trade and gold regions bordering present-day Mauritania.
Portuguese navigators reach the Mauritanian coast and establish a trading post at Arguin, opening European maritime contact.
Arguin becomes a center for the gum arabic, gold, and slave trade under Portuguese control.
The Char Bouba (Thirty Years') War begins, ending around 1674 with Arab Hassani warriors establishing dominance over the Berbers.
France establishes a trading presence on the coast, competing with other Europeans for the lucrative gum arabic trade.
The French frigate Méduse wrecks off the Mauritanian coast, an event that becomes infamous in European art and history.
French governor Louis Faidherbe extends Senegal-based influence northward into Mauritanian territory.
Xavier Coppolani begins France's 'peaceful penetration' campaign to bring the Mauritanian emirates under colonial control.
Coppolani is assassinated at Tidjikja, temporarily checking French expansion into the interior.
Mauritania is formally constituted as a territory within French West Africa.
France completes the military 'pacification' of Mauritania, subduing the last resisting nomadic groups.
Mauritania becomes a French overseas territory, gaining representation in the French Union.
Nouakchott is designated and begins development as the future national capital, having been little more than a small settlement.
Mauritania becomes an autonomous republic within the French Community following a referendum.
Mauritania achieves full independence from France on 28 November, with Moktar Ould Daddah as its first president.
Mauritania is admitted to the United Nations, despite Moroccan claims to the territory.
Mauritania joins the Arab League and introduces its own currency, the ouguiya, replacing the CFA franc.
Mauritania and Morocco agree to partition Spanish Sahara after Spain withdraws, drawing Mauritania into a costly war with the Polisario Front.
A military coup ousts President Moktar Ould Daddah, ending civilian rule amid the strains of the Western Sahara war.
Mauritania renounces its claim to Western Sahara and signs a peace agreement with the Polisario Front.
Mauritania formally abolishes slavery by decree, though the practice persists in many areas.
Colonel Maaouya Ould Sid'Ahmed Taya seizes power in a coup, beginning a long period of authoritarian rule.
The Mauritania-Senegal border conflict erupts, triggering violence and the expulsion of tens of thousands of black Mauritanians.
A new constitution introduces multiparty politics, ending the formal one-party era.
Mauritania withdraws from the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS).
A bloodless coup overthrows President Taya while he is abroad, with a military council promising a democratic transition.
Sidi Ould Cheikh Abdallahi wins Mauritania's first broadly recognized democratic presidential election.
Parliament passes a law criminalizing slavery for the first time, prescribing prison terms for slaveholders.
General Mohamed Ould Abdel Aziz leads a coup ousting the elected president, drawing international condemnation.
Abdel Aziz wins a presidential election, legitimizing his rule after the previous year's coup.
Mohamed Ould Ghazouani wins the presidency, marking Mauritania's first peaceful transfer of power between elected leaders.
President Ghazouani is re-elected, while offshore gas development and Sahel security pressures dominate national priorities.
